what_it_does: This file encodes a single operator memory rule as a Python dictionary named MEMORY: never create versioned copies of documents or artifacts, always update the same canonical file in place, and rely on the event log for history.
observations: The file is a data substrate with no executable code symbols.
The top docstring says the rule was migrated from harness notes and awaits Tim's confirmation.
The rule is attributed to Tim and dated 2026-06-01.
The MEMORY dict contains id, rule, why, evidence, scope, and status fields.
Evidence is a single quoted statement from a 'standing harness note'.
Scope applies whenever writing or updating any document/artifact.
standouts: The rule explicitly forbids v2, Round-N, dated, and '-updated' copies.
It mandates updating the SAME canonical file in place on every pass.
It is marked as operator memory awaiting confirmation but already described as all agents' knowledge.
concerns: Status is 'proposed' while the docstring says it has already been migrated to all agents' knowledge, creating a tension.
The evidence is a self-referential quote without an external, verifiable source.
The broad scope ('any document/artifact') may conflict with cases where versioning is genuinely useful.
There is no definition of 'the event log' or how it is accessed.
notes: A structured operator-memory entry that captures Tim's preference against AI-generated versioned file copies, currently in proposed status.
questions: Where is the event log and how do agents read it?
What happens if multiple agents concurrently update the same canonical file?
How does 'proposed' status get promoted or demoted?
Does this rule apply to code files, design docs, generated reports, or all of the above equally?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches: the file's stated purpose is to encode the no-versioning rule as operator memory, and that is exactly what the MEMORY dictionary does.
intention_signals: It is trying to turn a human operator's editorial correction into durable, shared agent knowledge so future passes do not create parallel file versions.
novelty: It frames a human preference as a structured memory object with evidence, scope, and status rather than as a plain comment or style guide line.
contribution: The explicit no-versioning rule and its rationale, plus the pattern of encoding operator preferences as structured, attributable memory entries.
summary_for_embedding: operator_memory/no_versioning.py stores a proposed operator rule forbidding versioned copies of artifacts and requiring in-place updates to a canonical file.
intention_for_embedding: To make Tim's no-versioning preference available as shared agent knowledge and prevent future AI passes from creating parallel file copies.
